strategy_notes: TRANSITIONING from pure defensive to balanced barbell approach. KEY EVOLUTION: (1) Sell defensives showing negative relative strength on strong rally days — executed with XOM and COST today. (2) Add cyclical/growth leaders with TIGHT stops (5%) — better to get stopped out than miss a genuine rotation. (3) Keep ABBV as defensive anchor (~12% of portfolio). (4) Maintain 40-45% cash for flexibility — if this is a bear bounce, cash protects; if genuine reversal, deploy next cycle. (5) CONFIRMATION CHECKLIST for next cycle: need 2+ consecutive up days with breadth expansion to add more cyclical exposure. A single-day reversal of >2% triggers immediate exit of new cyclical positions. (6) Improve win rate by using day-2 confirmation: if new positions are red on day 2, cut immediately rather than waiting for stop loss. (7) Accept that catching 60% of a move with tight risk management beats trying to catch 100% with loose stops.

lessons_learned.lesson: Relative strength signals on red market days proved reliable for entries, with AMD (+7.13%) and CVX (~+4.65%) delivering solid wins by buying names holding up during broad selloffs. However, the strategy showed a critical vulnerability: initial relative strength sometimes reversed immediately, as seen with RTX (-0.74%) and AVGO (-2.97%), both of which failed to confirm within days of entry. The key differentiator between wins and losses appears to be sector momentum durability — energy and tech (AMD) had genuine tailwinds, while defense and semiconductors faced swift sector-wide reversals. Disciplined profit-taking at the +5–7% threshold and early cutting of non-confirmers limited damage effectively. Going forward, prioritize confirming that the broader sector supports the trade, not just the individual name, and treat immediate post-entry reversal as an early exit signal rather than a reason to hold.

recent_trades_full.reason: ABBV +1.38% on a -1.18% SPY day — strong relative strength signal. Healthcare/pharma is a classic defensive sector that leads in the current phase of sector rotation (defensive-to-cyclical). ABBV has steady cash flows, strong pipeline, and dividend support. This is exactly the type of name my strategy calls for: quality defensive showing green on a red day. Moderate position size given still-bearish MACD environment.

COST +0.93% today on a red market day — relative strength in consumer staples/defensive retail. Costco is a high-quality defensive name that benefits from both consumer resilience and inflation hedging (membership model). Aligns with sector rotation research: defensives lead in correction environments. Smaller allocation to maintain cash reserves for potential deeper pullbacks.

MA is down -3.11% from entry and fell -2.71% today — the worst performer in the portfolio. The relative strength signal from entry has completely failed to confirm. V is also down -3.03% today, suggesting payment processors are rotating out with growth, not acting as defensives. This mirrors the RTX and AVGO pattern: initial relative strength that reverses immediately. Cutting before the -3% stop loss rule is breached. Following the evolved principle: cut non-confirmers by day 4.

JPM fell -1.97% today and is now -0.19% from entry. While it was a strong relative strength pick on entry day, financials are now selling off with the broader market. The relative strength signal is fading. With all MACD signals bearish and the defensive rotation clearly favoring staples/healthcare/energy over financials, I'm rotating out to redeploy into stronger defensive names. Better to take a small loss than let it widen.

XOM is the strongest name in the top 50 today at +1.84% on a deeply red day — textbook relative strength signal. Energy is showing persistent strength (CVX also +1.34%). Oil prices remain supported. XOM has strong cash flows and dividend yield. This is exactly my strategy: buy defensives/value showing green on red days. ATR-based sizing suggests larger position for lower-volatility energy names.

PEP is +1.24% today on a -0.84% SPY day — strong relative strength in consumer staples. PEP is a quality defensive name with pricing power, stable demand, and dividend support. Consumer staples are clearly leading the current rotation. PEP complements COST and ABBV for a defensive portfolio positioning. Moderate allocation per ATR-based volatility sizing — staples have lower volatility so can size slightly larger.

KO +1.37% on a -1.71% SPY day — strong relative strength in consumer staples. KO is one of the most defensive names in the market with pricing power, global diversification, and dividend aristocrat status. It complements PEP and COST in my staples-heavy defensive positioning. Adding a 5th position to maximize defensive exposure during this correction. Lower allocation to maintain ~30% cash reserves for potential deeper pullback.

PEP is -0.89% today on a +1.63% SPY day — significant underperformance. While +1.84% from entry, PEP is showing the weakest relative strength of all holdings on this rally day. If the market is transitioning from defensive to cyclical leadership, PEP will be the first to give back gains. Taking profit now to free up cash for potential rotation into higher-beta names if the transition confirms. Locking in a small gain rather than risk giving it back.

KO is -0.20% today on a +1.63% SPY day — the weakest relative strength in my portfolio. Only +0.39% gain from entry. KO is a pure defensive play that will underperform if this risk-on move has legs. Selling to raise cash and create portfolio flexibility. If the market reverses back down, I can re-enter KO or similar defensives. If the market continues rallying, I'll have cash to rotate into names showing confirmation of the trend change.

COST was flat (-0.02%) on a +2.91% SPY day — massive relative weakness. Consumer staples/defensive retail is rotating out hard. The +1.13% gain from entry is at risk of evaporating. Locking in small profit and freeing capital for better-positioned names.

XOM was -1.06% on a +2.91% SPY day — worst performer in the portfolio and one of the weakest in the entire universe today. Energy is selling off (CVX -1.81% too), suggesting geopolitical risk premium may be fading as markets rally. This is exactly the de-escalation exit signal my strategy notes flagged. Taking the +0.77% gain before it turns negative.

NVDA +5.59% today — leading the rally with strong semiconductor momentum. AI/datacenter secular tailwind remains intact. If this is a genuine recovery, semis will lead. If it's a bear bounce, the tight stop protects capital. Positioning for cyclical leadership rotation with controlled risk.

META +6.67% today — strongest performer in the top 50. Mega-cap tech is clearly leading this rotation. Strong fundamentals (ad revenue growth, AI investments, cost discipline). If breadth expansion continues over next 3 days, this confirms the trend. Tight stop to protect against bear trap reversal.
